name: Khidr
description: Khidr, often referred to as al-Khidr (the Green One), is a figure venerated in Islam as a prophet or saint possessing great wisdom and esoteric knowledge. His origins are shrouded in mystery, with some accounts linking him to pre-Islamic figures. He is believed to have drunk from the Spring of Life, granting him immortality or a prolonged lifespan, and is said to roam the earth guiding those who are lost and helping those in need. Khidr is frequently associated with figures of wisdom and guidance, and is often described as a mentor to seekers of truth. His most famous association is with the prophet Musa (Moses), as recounted in the Quran (Surah Al-Kahf), where Khidr demonstrates divine wisdom that transcends human understanding through seemingly unjust actions. These actions, however, are later revealed to have been for the greater good, illustrating Khidr's connection to divine will and his ability to perceive truths beyond the immediate. He is a symbol of divine grace, hidden knowledge, and the importance of trusting in the unseen hand of God. Khidr's stories emphasize that appearances can be deceiving and that there is often a deeper purpose behind events that may seem inexplicable or even wrong. He is a powerful figure of hope and guidance in Islamic spirituality.
subtitle: The Green One: Guide and Mystic

keyMyths.title: Meeting with Musa (Moses)
keyMyths.description: Khidr met Musa and tested his patience and understanding by performing seemingly unjust acts. He explained later that his actions were divinely guided to prevent greater harm, demonstrating a higher level of knowledge.

keyMyths.title: Drinking from the Spring of Life
keyMyths.description: It is believed that Khidr drank from the Spring of Life, granting him immortality or a very long life. This allows him to continue guiding and helping people throughout history.

keyMyths.title: The Hidden Guide
keyMyths.description: Khidr is often seen as a hidden guide who appears to those who are lost or in need of help. He provides guidance and support, often anonymously, to those who are worthy.

sources.author: Quran
sources.description: Contains the primary account of Khidr's interaction with Musa (Moses).
sources.text: Contains the primary account of Khidr's interaction with Musa (Moses).

sources.author: Various Sufi Authors
sources.description: References to Khidr's role as a spiritual guide and mystic are common in Sufi literature.
sources.text: References to Khidr's role as a spiritual guide and mystic are common in Sufi literature.
